questions.question_text: When must a Nevada licensee obtain a signed Consent to Act form from a client?
questions.correct_answer: Before performing any acts for the client
distractors: Within 48 hours of the first showing
At the time an offer is presented
Before the closing date is set
questions.explanation: Nevada law requires the Consent to Act form to be signed before the licensee performs any acts on behalf of the client. This ensures the client understands the agency relationship from the start. The Confirmation of Agency Relationship, not the Consent to Act, is provided at the time an offer is made.

questions.question_text: A Nevada buyer contacts a licensee about viewing properties. What does the Consent to Act form establish?
distractors: The commission rate to be paid
An employment agreement with the buyer
The geographic area of representation
questions.explanation: The Consent to Act form explains the nature of the agency relationship and discloses whether the licensee will act as an agent or limited agent. It does NOT create an employment agreement — it only confirms the agency. Commission rates and other employment terms are covered by separate brokerage agreements.

questions.question_text: A licensee shows properties to a buyer without first obtaining a Consent to Act. What is the licensee's status?
questions.correct_answer: In violation of Nevada licensing law
distractors: Automatically acting as a limited agent
Operating as a transaction broker
Representing the seller by default
questions.explanation: Nevada requires the Consent to Act form before any acts are performed on behalf of a client. Showing properties without this form violates NRS 645. The form must be obtained first to clarify the agency relationship before services begin.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Nevada's Consent to Act form is TRUE?
questions.correct_answer: It is not an employment agreement
distractors: It must be notarized to be valid
It guarantees the licensee a commission
It can only be used for sellers
questions.explanation: The Consent to Act confirms the agency relationship but does NOT create an employment agreement. It does not need to be notarized, does not guarantee compensation, and is required for both buyer and seller clients. Separate brokerage agreements address employment and compensation terms.

questions.question_text: A licensee plans to represent both buyer and seller with their consent. What must the Consent to Act form disclose?
questions.correct_answer: That the licensee will act as a limited agent
distractors: That the seller will pay both commissions
That neither party will have representation
That the transaction requires an attorney
questions.explanation: When a licensee represents both parties, the Consent to Act form must disclose that the licensee will act as a limited agent (disclosed dual agent). Both parties must provide informed written consent. The form clarifies the nature of the relationship so both parties understand the dual representation.

questions.question_text: When must a Nevada licensee provide the Confirmation of Agency Relationship?
questions.correct_answer: At the time an offer is made
distractors: At the first property showing
Before listing the property
Within 10 days of closing
questions.explanation: The Confirmation of Agency Relationship is provided at the time an offer is made. This is distinct from the Consent to Act, which must be signed before any acts are performed. The Confirmation confirms the established relationships of all agents in the transaction.

questions.question_text: What does Nevada's Confirmation of Agency Relationship confirm?
questions.correct_answer: Each agent's relationship to their party
distractors: The final purchase price of the property
The commission split between brokerages
The buyer's financing pre-approval status
questions.explanation: The Confirmation of Agency Relationship confirms the listing agent's relationship to the seller, the selling agent's relationship to the buyer, and whether dual agency (limited agency) exists. It does not address price, commissions, or financing details.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent submits an offer on a property listed by the same brokerage. The Confirmation of Agency Relationship must disclose which situation?
distractors: That the buyer waives representation
That the listing must be withdrawn
That an outside broker must mediate
questions.explanation: When both sides of a transaction are handled by the same brokerage, limited agency (dual agency) exists. The Confirmation of Agency Relationship must disclose this to both parties. Nevada calls dual agency 'limited agency' because the licensee's duties to each party are limited by the dual representation.

questions.question_text: Which parties must sign the Confirmation of Agency Relationship in a Nevada transaction?
distractors: Only the buyer and buyer's agent
Only the seller and listing agent
Only the two brokers involved
questions.explanation: The Confirmation of Agency Relationship must be signed by both parties (buyer and seller) and both licensees (listing agent and selling agent). This ensures all parties acknowledge and confirm the agency relationships in the transaction.

questions.question_text: How does the Confirmation of Agency Relationship differ from the Consent to Act in Nevada?
questions.correct_answer: Confirmation is at offer; Consent is before services
distractors: Confirmation replaces the Consent to Act
Consent is optional; Confirmation is required
Confirmation is only for dual agency situations
questions.explanation: The Consent to Act must be signed before the licensee performs any acts on behalf of a client, while the Confirmation of Agency Relationship is provided at the time an offer is made. Both forms are required — the Consent establishes the relationship, and the Confirmation verifies it for all parties in the transaction.
